title: Cedar Planters with 2x2 Legs
summary: A footed cedar planter built as four picket panels pocket-screwed to 2x2 legs — with a false bottom, because no plant needs two feet of dirt.
description:
This planter is a lesson in building with imperfect material. Cedar fence pickets are cheap because they're rough, wet, and inconsistent — so the design plans for it: the panels are sized with 1/8" gaps between pickets to absorb seasonal movement, and the panel-on-legs construction means no picket edge ever has to land perfectly against another.

The build order is what makes it fast: assemble four flat panels first (pickets nailed to 1x3 rails), then pocket-screw the panels to the 2x2 legs from the inside. All the joinery is hidden, and a panel that comes out slightly out of square gets pulled true when it meets the legs.

The best idea in the plan is the false bottom. Don't fill a 24"-tall planter with soil — you'd waste money on dirt, and the thing would weigh enough to crack a deck board. Set cleats about 6" down from the rim, drop a slatted floor on them, and plant in that. Lighter, cheaper, and it drains better than a full column of soil ever would.

tools.note: 1-1/2" pocket holes join the panels to the legs — the whole structure, hidden inside.
Or hand-nail with galvanized nails — the panels just need pickets held to rails while the glue cures.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: Twelve panel pickets from three boards.

cutList.note: Two per panel, plus two spares for the false-bottom cleats.
1/4" shorter than the diagrams to allow for the saw kerf and trimming off the dog-eared ends.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut ten panel rails at 17" from the 1x3. Cut five false-bottom cleats and slats at 17" from the 1x2. Cut four legs at 23-3/4" from the 2x2. Cut twelve panel pickets at 17-3/4" from the Cedar fence picket. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 1x3, one 8 ft 1x2 and one 8 ft 2x2. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the four panels
steps.body:
Nail and glue three pickets to two 1x3 rails per panel, working from the inside face so the fasteners don't show. Leave an 1/8" gap between pickets — cedar pickets are wet off the rack and WILL move, and the gap is where that movement goes instead of into a bowed panel.

Don't chase perfection here. The pickets aren't perfect and the design knows it.

steps.title: Drill the pocket holes
steps.body: On the inside of every panel, drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes behind each 1x3 rail end. The screws will run through picket and rail into the legs — that stack is the whole joint, so drill at the rail ends where the material is.

steps.title: Join two panels to the legs
steps.body: Attach two opposite panels to the four legs with the 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws, panels flush to the leg faces per the diagram. Work on a flat surface so the legs all land level.

steps.title: Close the box and square it
steps.body: Add the remaining two panels the same way. Before the final screws go in, check the planter for square across the top opening and rack it true — pocket screws allow one round of adjustment, then they're committed.

steps.title: Install the false bottom
steps.body:
You are not growing potatoes — don't fill the whole planter with dirt. Screw 1x2 cleats around the inside about 6" down from the rim (or deeper for bigger root balls), then lay 1x2 slats across them with drainage gaps.

Line with landscape fabric to keep soil off the cedar, plant, and set it somewhere it gets admired. Sand and break the top edges first — hands land on the rim every time someone waters it.

images.alt: Square cedar planter with picket panel sides raised on 2x2 legs
